questions.question:
Which one of the following uses UDP as the transport protocol?
A. HTTP
B. Telnet
C. DNS
D. SMTP
questions.to_find: Which application-layer protocol uses UDP as its transport protocol

understand.plain: Different application-layer protocols choose either TCP or UDP at the transport layer. TCP provides reliable, connection-oriented delivery; UDP is connectionless and faster but unreliable. DNS primarily uses UDP (port 53) for quick query-response lookups, though it can fall back to TCP for large responses or zone transfers. HTTP, Telnet, and SMTP all require reliable byte-stream delivery and thus use TCP.

keywords.explain: A connectionless, unreliable transport protocol that has low overhead and is fast, suitable for applications that can tolerate some packet loss.
keywords.example: DNS query: a client sends a single UDP packet asking for an IP address and gets a single UDP reply.

keywords.explain: A protocol that maps human-readable domain names (like www.example.com) to IP addresses. It uses UDP port 53 for most queries.
keywords.example: Typing 'google.com' in a browser triggers a DNS UDP query to find Google's IP address.

understand.visual_alt: Transport protocol usage: HTTP->TCP, Telnet->TCP, DNS->UDP, SMTP->TCP

given.aim: Identify which protocol among HTTP, Telnet, DNS, SMTP uses UDP

terms.meaning: HyperText Transfer Protocol for web pages
terms.connects: Uses TCP (port 80/443) for reliable delivery of web content

terms.example: Logging into a remote machine's shell
terms.connects: Uses TCP (port 23) because interactive sessions need reliable byte stream

terms.meaning: Simple Mail Transfer Protocol for sending emails
terms.example: Sending an email from a mail client
terms.connects: Uses TCP (port 25) for reliable email delivery

terms.meaning: Domain Name System for name-to-IP resolution
terms.connects: Uses UDP (port 53) for fast, low-overhead name lookups

given.plan: Check each protocol's standard transport layer: HTTP, Telnet, SMTP all use TCP for reliability. DNS uses UDP for its short query-response interactions to minimize latency.

steps.title: Check HTTP, Telnet, SMTP
steps.apply: HTTP runs over TCP (ports 80/443). Telnet runs over TCP (port 23). SMTP runs over TCP (port 25). All three require reliable, ordered delivery that TCP provides.
steps.note: These protocols all involve sustained, stateful connections where lost data would be catastrophic.

steps.title: Check DNS
steps.apply: DNS uses UDP (port 53) for standard queries. The request and reply each fit in a single small packet. The overhead of establishing a TCP connection would be wasteful for such short exchanges. DNS uses UDP because speed matters more than guaranteed delivery for name resolution.
steps.note: DNS can use TCP for zone transfers or when response size exceeds 512 bytes (EDNS extends this), but standard queries use UDP.

solution.result: DNS (option C) uses UDP as the transport protocol.

questions.formula_note: Application-layer protocol transport mappings: DNS uses UDP port 53; HTTP, Telnet, SMTP use TCP.

questions.question:
In one of the pairs of protocols given below, both the protocols can use multiple TCP connections between the same client and the server. Which one is that?
A. HTTP, FTP
B. HTTP, TELNET
C. FTP, SMTP
D. HTTP, SMTP
questions.to_find: Which pair of protocols both support multiple simultaneous TCP connections between client and server

understand.plain: Some application-layer protocols open multiple TCP connections for different purposes. HTTP can open multiple parallel connections to fetch multiple resources simultaneously. FTP uses at least two connections: one for control (port 21) and one or more for data transfers (port 20 or ephemeral ports). Telnet and SMTP typically use a single TCP connection for their entire session.

keywords.explain: A protocol that uses two separate TCP connections: a control connection (port 21) that persists for the session, and a separate data connection (port 20 or negotiated) for each file transfer.
keywords.example: When you download 3 files via FTP, there is 1 control connection and up to 3 data connections.

keywords.explain: HTTP/1.0 opens a new TCP connection for each object (non-persistent). HTTP/1.1 introduced persistent connections and pipelining, and browsers also open multiple parallel TCP connections to speed up page loading.
keywords.example: A browser fetching a webpage with 10 images may open 6 parallel TCP connections to the same server.

understand.visual_alt: HTTP uses multiple parallel connections; FTP uses separate control and data connections; both use multiple TCP connections to the same server.

given.aim: Find the protocol pair where both protocols can use multiple TCP connections to the same server

terms.meaning: Browsers open multiple TCP connections in parallel to the same web server to fetch resources concurrently
terms.example: Chrome opens up to 6 connections per domain
terms.connects: Qualifies HTTP as using multiple TCP connections

terms.meaning: FTP always uses at least 2 TCP connections: control (port 21) and data (port 20 or negotiated)
terms.example: Downloading 3 files = 1 control + 3 data TCP connections
terms.connects: Qualifies FTP as using multiple TCP connections

terms.meaning: SMTP sends all mail messages over a single persistent TCP connection to the mail server
terms.example: Sending 5 emails in one session uses one TCP connection

terms.meaning: Telnet establishes one TCP connection for the entire interactive terminal session
terms.example: A remote login session is one TCP connection

given.plan: Analyze each protocol's connection model. Both HTTP and FTP inherently use multiple TCP connections (HTTP for parallel fetching, FTP for separate control and data channels), making option A correct.

steps.title: Analyze HTTP connection behavior
steps.apply: HTTP/1.0: Opens a new TCP connection per object (non-persistent). HTTP/1.1: Supports persistent connections but browsers still open multiple parallel TCP connections (typically 6) to the same server for performance. So HTTP can use multiple TCP connections to the same server.
steps.note: Even with HTTP/1.1 persistent connections, browsers use multiple parallel connections to the same host.

steps.title: Analyze FTP connection behavior
steps.apply: FTP always maintains a persistent control connection (port 21) and opens a separate data connection (port 20 in active mode, or a negotiated port in passive mode) for each file transfer. So FTP explicitly uses multiple TCP connections to the same server simultaneously.
steps.note: The two-connection model is a fundamental part of FTP's design.

steps.title: Eliminate other options
steps.apply: Telnet: Uses exactly one TCP connection for the interactive terminal session. SMTP: Uses one TCP connection to deliver mail. Neither Telnet nor SMTP uses multiple TCP connections to the same server, so options B, C, D are eliminated.
steps.note: B has Telnet (single connection), C has SMTP (single connection), D has SMTP (single connection).

solution.result: HTTP, FTP (option A) — both protocols can use multiple TCP connections between the same client and server.

questions.formula_note: FTP uses two types of connections: control (port 21) and data (port 20); HTTP browsers use multiple parallel TCP connections for performance.

questions.question:
Which one of the following protocols is NOT used to resolve one form of address to another one?
A. DNS
B. ARP
C. DHCP
D. RARP
questions.to_find: Which protocol does NOT perform address resolution (mapping one address form to another)

understand.plain: Address resolution means converting one type of address into another type. DNS converts domain names to IP addresses. ARP converts IP addresses to MAC addresses. RARP converts MAC addresses to IP addresses. DHCP, however, does not resolve addresses — it dynamically assigns IP addresses to hosts from a pool, which is address assignment/configuration, not resolution.

keywords.explain: The process of mapping one type of network address to another, such as hostname to IP, or IP to MAC.
A protocol that automatically assigns IP addresses, subnet masks, gateways, and DNS server addresses to hosts on a network. It allocates, not resolves, addresses.
keywords.example: When you connect to Wi-Fi, DHCP assigns your laptop an IP like 192.168.0.105 from the router's pool.

understand.visual_alt: Address resolution chain: DNS (name->IP), ARP (IP->MAC), RARP (MAC->IP). DHCP assigns IP addresses dynamically — not resolution.

given.aim: Identify the protocol that does NOT map one form of address to another

terms.meaning: Resolves domain names (hostnames) to IP addresses
terms.connects: DNS IS an address resolution protocol

terms.meaning: Resolves IPv4 addresses to MAC (hardware) addresses on a local network
terms.connects: ARP IS an address resolution protocol

terms.meaning: Resolves MAC addresses to IP addresses (the reverse of ARP), used by diskless workstations to learn their IP
terms.connects: RARP IS an address resolution protocol

terms.meaning: Dynamically assigns configuration parameters including IP addresses to hosts
terms.example: Host broadcasts DHCP Discover; server responds with an IP address lease
terms.connects: DHCP assigns addresses — it does NOT resolve one address form to another, so it is NOT a resolution protocol

given.plan: Check each protocol's purpose: DNS, ARP, and RARP all map one address form to another (resolution). DHCP allocates IP addresses dynamically — it is a configuration protocol, not a resolution protocol.

steps.title: Verify DNS, ARP, RARP as address resolution protocols
steps.apply: DNS: hostname -> IP address (layer 7 name resolution). ARP: IP -> MAC address (layer 3 to layer 2 resolution). RARP: MAC -> IP address (layer 2 to layer 3 reverse resolution). All three convert one form of address to another form.
steps.note: RARP is largely superseded by BOOTP and DHCP today, but it was indeed an address resolution protocol.

steps.title: Evaluate DHCP
steps.apply: DHCP dynamically assigns IP addresses (and other network parameters) to hosts from a configured pool. A client broadcasts a request; the server leases an IP address to the client. DHCP does not convert one existing address form to another — it creates/assigns addresses. Therefore DHCP is NOT an address resolution protocol.
steps.note: DHCP can also provide the address of a DNS server, but that is configuration delivery, not address resolution.

solution.result: DHCP (option C) is NOT used to resolve one form of address to another — it assigns IP addresses dynamically.

questions.formula_note: Address resolution protocols: DNS (name->IP), ARP (IP->MAC), RARP (MAC->IP). DHCP performs dynamic address assignment, not resolution.

questions.question:
Suppose that in an IP-over-Ethernet network, a machine X wishes to find the MAC address of another machine Y in its subnet. Which one of the following techniques can be used for this?
A. X sends an ARP request packet to the local gateway's IP address which then finds the MAC address of Y and sends to X.
B. X sends an ARP request packet to the local gateway's MAC address which then finds the MAC address of Y and sends to X.
C. X sends an ARP request packet with broadcast IP address in its local subnet.
D. X sends an ARP request packet with broadcast MAC address in its local subnet.
questions.answer: D. X sends an ARP request packet with broadcast MAC address in its local subnet.
questions.to_find: The correct technique machine X uses to find the MAC address of machine Y in its subnet via ARP

understand.plain: ARP (Address Resolution Protocol) is used to find a MAC address when only the IP address is known. When X wants Y's MAC address, X broadcasts an ARP request on the local network. The broadcast is sent to the Ethernet broadcast MAC address (FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF), not to any IP address. Every host on the subnet receives the broadcast, and Y recognizes its own IP and replies with its MAC address.

keywords.explain: A broadcast Ethernet frame asking 'Who has IP address X.X.X.X? Tell me your MAC address.' The destination MAC is FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF (Ethernet broadcast).
keywords.example: X broadcasts: 'Who has 192.168.1.10?' Y responds: 'I have it, my MAC is AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF.'

keywords.explain: A special MAC address that causes all devices on the local Ethernet segment to receive and process the frame.
keywords.example: ARP requests are sent to FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF so every machine on the subnet sees the request.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 480 200' width='480' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='20' y='60' width='80' height='60' class='hi' rx='8'/><text x='60' y='86' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>X</text><text x='60' y='106' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>192.168.1.5</text><rect x='380' y='60' width='80' height='60' class='hi' rx='8'/><text x='420' y='86' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Y</text><text x='420' y='106' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>192.168.1.10</text><rect x='180' y='130' width='120' height='40' class='bg' rx='6'/><text x='240' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Other hosts</text><line x1='100' y1='110' x2='380' y2='110' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='240' y1='130' x2='240' y2='110' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><text x='240' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>ARP Request: dst MAC = FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF</text><text x='240' y='50' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>'Who has 192.168.1.10?'</text><polygon points='370,105 380,110 370,115' fill='#7F77DD'/></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Machine X sends an ARP request with destination MAC FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF (broadcast). All hosts on the subnet receive it. Y sees its own IP and sends a unicast ARP reply with its MAC address back to X.

given.aim: Find how machine X discovers the MAC address of machine Y on the same subnet using ARP

terms.meaning: Protocol for mapping a known IP address to an unknown MAC address within a local subnet
terms.example: To send an Ethernet frame to 192.168.1.10, X needs its MAC address — ARP finds it
terms.connects: ARP is the mechanism X uses to find Y's MAC

terms.meaning: Sending a frame to MAC address FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF, received by all devices on the local Ethernet segment
terms.example: ARP request goes to broadcast so every host on the subnet sees the request
terms.connects: ARP request destination is the Ethernet broadcast MAC, not an IP broadcast

terms.meaning: When X and Y are on the same subnet, X can reach Y directly without involving the gateway
terms.example: Both X (192.168.1.5) and Y (192.168.1.10) are in 192.168.1.0/24 — no routing needed
terms.connects: Eliminates options A and B which involve the gateway unnecessarily

given.plan: ARP requests are Ethernet broadcasts (destination MAC = FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF). They are NOT routed to the gateway. Options A and B incorrectly involve the gateway. Option C incorrectly uses IP broadcast instead of MAC broadcast. Option D correctly describes ARP: broadcast using the Ethernet broadcast MAC address.

steps.title: Eliminate options A and B (gateway involvement)
steps.apply: ARP operates at Layer 2 (Ethernet) within a single subnet. The gateway is not involved when X and Y are on the same subnet. Options A and B involve sending to the gateway's IP or MAC address respectively, which is incorrect — the gateway only gets involved for cross-subnet routing, not for local ARP.
steps.note: ARP is a Layer 2 protocol; it does not cross router boundaries.

steps.title: Evaluate option C (IP broadcast)
steps.apply: Option C says X sends the ARP request to the 'broadcast IP address'. ARP works at the Ethernet (Layer 2) level. The destination field in an ARP request is set to the Ethernet broadcast MAC (FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF), not an IP broadcast. The IP layer is not used to address ARP frames.
steps.note: ARP packets are encapsulated directly in Ethernet frames, not in IP datagrams.

steps.title: Confirm option D (Ethernet broadcast MAC)
steps.apply: X constructs an ARP request packet containing: sender IP (X's IP), sender MAC (X's MAC), target IP (Y's IP), target MAC (unknown, set to 00:00:00:00:00:00). This is sent in an Ethernet frame with destination MAC = FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF (broadcast). All hosts on the subnet receive it. Y recognizes its own IP, and sends a unicast ARP reply to X with its MAC address.
steps.note: This is the standard ARP procedure defined in RFC 826.

solution.result: Option D: X sends an ARP request packet with broadcast MAC address (FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF) in its local subnet.

questions.formula_note: ARP (RFC 826): request is broadcast over Ethernet (dst MAC = FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF); reply is unicast. ARP operates within a single subnet without involving the gateway.

questions.question:
Consider the sliding window flow-control protocol operating between a sender and a receiver over a full-duplex error-free link. Assume the following:
- The time taken for processing the data frame by the receiver is negligible.
- The time taken for processing the acknowledgement frame by the sender is negligible.
- The sender has infinite number of frames available for transmission.
- The size of the data frame is 2,000 bits and the size of the acknowledgement frame is 10 bits.
- The link data rate in each direction is 1 Mbps (= 10^6 bits per second).
- The propagation delay of the link is 100 milliseconds.

The minimum number of frames, the window size (rounded to the nearest integer) needed to achieve a link utilization of 50% is ___ .
questions.to_find: Minimum window size (number of frames) needed to achieve 50% link utilization

understand.plain: In a sliding window protocol, the sender can have multiple unacknowledged frames 'in flight'. Link utilization depends on how many frames can be in transit during one round-trip time. If the window is too small, the sender must stop and wait for ACKs, wasting bandwidth. We need to find the smallest window size that keeps the link at least 50% utilized, accounting for both the data frame and ACK transmission times.

keywords.explain: The fraction of time the sender is actually transmitting useful data. Efficiency = (W * Tt) / (Tt + 2*Tp + Tack) where W is window size, Tt is transmission time per frame, Tp is propagation delay, Tack is ACK transmission time.
keywords.example: If Tt = 2ms, Tp = 100ms, Tack = 0.01ms, and W = 51, efficiency = 51*2 / (2 + 200 + 0.01) >= 50%.

keywords.explain: A flow control mechanism where the sender can transmit up to W frames before receiving an acknowledgement, keeping the link busy during propagation delays.
keywords.example: With W=1, sender transmits 1 frame then waits 200+ms for ACK; with W=51, it keeps sending while waiting.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 500 220' width='500' height='220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='250' y='25' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Sliding Window Timing</text><rect x='10' y='40' width='90' height='30' class='hi' rx='4'/><text x='55' y='60' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Sender</text><rect x='400' y='40' width='90' height='30' class='hi' rx='4'/><text x='445' y='60' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Receiver</text><line x1='55' y1='80' x2='55' y2='200' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='4'/><line x1='445' y1='80' x2='445' y2='200' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='4'/><line x1='55' y1='90' x2='445' y2='140' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='250' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>data frame (Tt=2ms, Tp=100ms)</text><line x1='445' y1='150' x2='55' y2='195' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><text x='250' y='180' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>ACK (Tack=0.01ms, Tp=100ms)</text><text x='55' y='215' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>RTT = Tt + 2Tp + Tack = 202.01ms</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Timeline: sender transmits frame (Tt=2ms), frame propagates (Tp=100ms), receiver processes (0ms), ACK transmits (0.01ms), ACK propagates back (100ms). Total cycle = ~202ms. Window must cover 50% of cycle.

given.aim: Find minimum window size W such that link utilization >= 50%

terms.meaning: Time to put all bits of a frame onto the link = frame_size / link_rate
Time for a signal to travel from sender to receiver = 100 ms (given)
terms.example: Signal travels 100ms one-way, so 200ms round-trip propagation
terms.connects: Dominates the denominator of efficiency formula

terms.meaning: Time to transmit the acknowledgement frame = 10 bits / 10^6 bps = 0.01 ms
terms.example: Very small compared to Tt and Tp
terms.connects: Contributes to the denominator of efficiency formula

terms.meaning: Fraction of time the sender is usefully transmitting = W * Tt / (Tt + 2*Tp + Tack)
terms.connects: Must be >= 0.50 (50%), solve for minimum W

given.plan: Calculate Tt, Tp, Tack. Apply the sliding window efficiency formula: utilization = W * Tt / (Tt + 2*Tp + Tack) >= 0.50. Solve for W, then round up to nearest integer.

steps.title: Calculate individual time components
steps.formula_raw: Tt = frame_size / link_rate, Tack = ack_size / link_rate
steps.apply:
Tt = 2000 / 10^6 = 2 ms
Tp = 100 ms (given)
Tack = 10 / 10^6 = 0.01 ms
Total round-trip cycle time = Tt + 2*Tp + Tack = 2 + 200 + 0.01 = 202.01 ms
steps.note: The round-trip cycle is dominated by the 200ms propagation round-trip.

steps.title: Apply efficiency formula and solve for W
steps.note: We need W >= 50.5025, so the minimum integer W is 51. (50 would give utilization = 50*2/202.01 = 49.5% < 50%.)

steps.title: Verify with W = 51
steps.apply:
Utilization = 102 / 202.01 = 0.5049 = 50.49% >= 50%. Confirmed.
With W = 50: utilization = 100 / 202.01 = 49.5% < 50%. Not sufficient.
steps.note: W = 51 is the minimum window size achieving at least 50% utilization.

solution.result: The minimum window size is 51 frames.

questions.formula_note: Sliding window efficiency formula: Efficiency = W * Tt / (Tt + 2*Tp + Tack), where W is window size, Tt is data frame transmission time, Tp is one-way propagation delay, Tack is ACK transmission time.
